summary: A partially identifies pairwise averaging and mentions shared memory/divide-and-conquer but omits threadgroup_barrier, log2(N) complexity, and multi-threadgroup strategy. B commits a fatal misidentification (CNN/Max Pooling), has zero required facts, and triggers every critical error. A is less wrong.

Both answers are truncated mid-sentence and cover only two required facts: the 50% resolution ceiling and Route A's 36% effective yield. Neither completes Route B's yield calculation (~39.4%), names the sunk cost fallacy, identifies the $350k as irrelevant, nor connects the bias to R&D misallocation. No critical errors, but both are critically incomplete.

Both answers entirely ignore the 17th-century register requirement, writing in plain modern prose with no archaic syntax, spelling, or vocabulary—a disqualifying critical error. A marginally better anchors encoder zeroing at the home datum; B muddles that step. Neither earns points for linguistic compliance.

Both answers are truncated mid-sentence and cover none of the required facts. Neither identifies -est false positives, hath/doth omission, thine predicate-position failure, nor proposes an irregular-forms pre-pass. The only difference is a missing space in B; content is otherwise identical and equally deficient.
